questions.question: Explain the DDA algorithm and its primary purpose in computer graphics.
keyPoints: DDA stands for Digital Differential Analyzer
Used for line drawing in raster display systems
Converts continuous mathematical lines to discrete pixels
Incremental calculation approach

questions.question: With the help of a flowchart, explain the step-by-step procedure of the DDA algorithm.
keyPoints: Calculate slope m = (y2-y1)/(x2-x1)
Determine step direction based on |m|
Initialize starting point
Iteratively calculate next positions using increments
Round to nearest integer and plot
Continue until endpoint reached

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of the DDA algorithm?
keyPoints: Advantages: Simple to understand and implement, Conceptually clear, Works well for short lines
Disadvantages: Uses expensive floating-point arithmetic, Accumulates rounding errors, Slower than integer algorithms, Error increases with line length

questions.question: Differentiate between DDA algorithm and Bresenham's line algorithm.
keyPoints: DDA uses floating-point arithmetic, Bresenham uses only integers
Bresenham is faster and more efficient
DDA accumulates rounding errors, Bresenham has error checking without floating-point
Bresenham produces more accurate results for longer lines
DDA is simpler to understand conceptually

questions.question: Write the DDA algorithm for drawing a line from (x1,y1) to (x2,y2).
keyPoints: Calculate dx = x2-x1, dy = y2-y1
Calculate steps = max(|dx|,|dy|)
Calculate x-increment = dx/steps, y-increment = dy/steps
Initialize x = x1, y = y1
Plot (round(x), round(y))
Repeat for steps: x += x-increment, y += y-increment, plot

questions.question: Explain how DDA handles lines with different slopes (0<|m|<1, |m|>1, m=0, vertical lines).
keyPoints: For 0<|m|<1: step in x-direction, increment y by m
For |m|>1: step in y-direction, increment x by 1/m
For m=0 (horizontal): increment x by 1, y stays constant
For vertical lines: increment y by 1, x stays constant

questions.question: Why is the DDA algorithm considered inefficient? Explain with reason.
keyPoints: Uses floating-point arithmetic which is computationally expensive
Multiplication and division operations are slower than integer operations
Rounding operations add overhead
Better alternatives like Bresenham use only integer operations
Memory access patterns are similar but computational cost differs

questions.question: Apply the DDA algorithm to draw a line from point (2,3) to (10,11). Show all intermediate steps and the pixels plotted.
keyPoints: Calculate dx=8, dy=8, m=1
Steps = max(8,8) = 8
x-increment = 8/8 = 1, y-increment = 8/8 = 1
Start: (2,3) → plot (2,3)
Continue similarly...
End at (10,11)
Since m=1, this is a diagonal line at 45°

questions.question: Compare DDA and Bresenham's line drawing algorithms under the following headings: (i) Arithmetic used (ii) Speed (iii) Accuracy (iv) Error accumulation (v) Complexity
keyPoints: (i) DDA uses floating-point, Bresenham uses integer only
(ii) Bresenham is faster due to integer operations
(iii) Bresenham more accurate, especially for long lines
(iv) DDA accumulates rounding errors, Bresenham has controlled error
(v) Both O(n) time complexity, O(1) space complexity
DDA simpler to understand, Bresenham more practical

questions.question: Write a complete pseudocode for the DDA line drawing algorithm and explain each step. Also analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Pseudocode with: input endpoints, calculate dx, dy, m, steps
Determine xincrement and yincrement
Initialize x, y
Loop through steps
Round and plot each pixel
Update x, y
Time complexity: O(max(|dx|,|dy|)) proportional to line length
Space complexity: O(1) - only fixed variables used
Explanation of each step

questions.question: Explain why DDA algorithm produces errors for long lines. How does Bresenham's algorithm overcome this limitation?
keyPoints: DDA uses floating-point with rounding at each step
Small rounding errors accumulate over many iterations
For long lines, accumulated error can cause visible drift from true line
Bresenham uses integer arithmetic with error term
Error term is checked at each step without floating-point
Decision based on sign of error term only
No accumulation of floating-point errors
Integer operations are exact, no rounding needed
